content.text: activates a family of eight type-safety checks in one switch. Angular CLI has generated projects with strict mode on by default since Angular 12. Understanding what each flag does — and why Angular adds its own on top — lets you reason about errors precisely and opt out surgically when necessary.

@Input properties cannot be readonly or private

content.text: Enabling Strict Mode in an Existing Project
in a brownfield project typically surfaces many errors at once. The recommended approach is to enable flags incrementally:
— it catches the most bugs and its errors are mechanical to fix (add
on lines you cannot fix yet — but track them and remove them as you go.
to see the full error list without producing output files.
preserves your existing tsconfig; you add strictness on top yourself.
New Angular CLI projects start with all flags enabled — do not turn them off unless you have a concrete reason.
Sometimes a single file or line needs to escape a strict check. Use these escape hatches sparingly and comment why:

Initialize in declaration or constructor; use ! for @Input
Widen parameter type or use exact match
Variable 'e' in catch clause is of type 'unknown'

content.text: enables eight TypeScript checks: strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny, strictPropertyInitialization, strictFunctionTypes, strictBindCallApply, noImplicitThis, alwaysStrict, and useUnknownInCatchVariables.
Angular CLI generates projects with strict mode on by default since Angular 12 — leave it enabled for new projects.
Angular adds three extra flags in angularCompilerOptions: strictTemplates, strictInjectionParameters, and strictInputAccessModifiers.
Migrate brownfield projects incrementally: enable one flag at a time, fix errors, and commit before adding the next flag.
Opt out at the file or line level with @ts-nocheck or @ts-ignore only when necessary, and always leave a comment explaining why.
